source.title: Design Patterns: Elements of Reusable Object-Oriented Software

pipeline_steps.name: Creational
pipeline_steps.description: Patterns that deal with object creation mechanisms

pipeline_steps.name: Structural
pipeline_steps.description: Patterns that identify simple ways to realize relationships among entities

pipeline_steps.name: Behavioral
pipeline_steps.description: Patterns that identify common communication patterns among objects

pipeline_steps.name: Concurrency
pipeline_steps.description: Patterns for multi-threaded and parallel programming

methods.name: Singleton
methods.short_description: Ensure a class has only one instance and provide a global point of access to it.
methods.algorithm_summary: The Singleton pattern restricts instantiation of a class to a single object. It involves a private constructor, a static method that returns the instance, and a static variable holding the single instance. Lazy initialization creates the instance on first access; eager initialization creates it at class load time. Thread-safe variants use double-checked locking or initialization-on-demand holder patterns.
assumptions: Only one instance is needed throughout the application
limitations: Difficult to unit test due to global state
Violates Single Responsibility Principle
Hidden dependencies in code

references.paper_title: Design Patterns: Elements of Reusable Object-Oriented Software

methods.name: Factory Method
methods.short_description: Define an interface for creating an object, but let subclasses decide which class to instantiate.
methods.algorithm_summary: The Factory Method pattern defines a method in a creator class that returns a product object. Subclasses override this method to produce different products. The creator class works with products through the common interface, allowing new product types to be introduced without modifying the creator. This promotes loose coupling between the creator and concrete products.
assumptions: Creation logic may vary across subclasses
limitations: Can lead to many subclasses
Adds complexity for simple creation scenarios

references.paper_title: Design Patterns: Elements of Reusable Object-Oriented Software

methods.name: Abstract Factory
methods.short_description: Provide an interface for creating families of related or dependent objects without specifying their concrete classes.
methods.algorithm_summary: The Abstract Factory pattern provides an interface that creates families of related objects without specifying their concrete classes. Each concrete factory implements the creation methods for a particular product family. Client code works with factories and products only through their abstract interfaces, making it easy to swap entire product families by changing the concrete factory.
assumptions: Products come in families that should be used together
System should be independent of how products are created
limitations: Difficult to add new product types
High number of interfaces and classes

references.paper_title: Design Patterns: Elements of Reusable Object-Oriented Software

methods.name: Builder
methods.short_description: Separate the construction of a complex object from its representation so that the same construction process can create different representations.
methods.algorithm_summary: The Builder pattern separates the construction of a complex object from its representation. A director class defines the order of construction steps, while concrete builder classes implement those steps differently. The client configures the director with the desired builder, and the director orchestrates the build process. This allows the same construction process to produce different representations.
assumptions: Different representations of the same object are needed
limitations: Increases overall code complexity
Requires creating separate builder for each product type

references.paper_title: Design Patterns: Elements of Reusable Object-Oriented Software

methods.name: Prototype
methods.short_description: Specify the kinds of objects to create using a prototypical instance, and create new objects by copying this prototype.
methods.algorithm_summary: The Prototype pattern creates new objects by cloning an existing object (the prototype) rather than using constructors. It defines a clone interface that all prototypical objects implement. A registry or manager can store commonly used prototypes. Deep vs. shallow copy considerations are important, especially for objects containing references to other objects.
limitations: Deep cloning complex objects can be difficult
Circular references require special handling

references.paper_title: Design Patterns: Elements of Reusable Object-Oriented Software

methods.name: Adapter
methods.short_description: Convert the interface of a class into another interface clients expect, letting classes work together that couldn't otherwise due to incompatible interfaces.
methods.algorithm_summary: The Adapter pattern wraps an existing class with a new interface. A class adapter uses multiple inheritance (where available) to adapt one interface to another. An object adapter uses composition, holding a reference to the adaptee. The adapter translates calls from the target interface into calls on the adaptee, bridging incompatible interfaces without modifying existing code.
limitations: Adds a layer of indirection
Can obscure the actual API being used

references.paper_title: Design Patterns: Elements of Reusable Object-Oriented Software

methods.name: Decorator
methods.short_description: Attach additional responsibilities to an object dynamically, providing a flexible alternative to subclassing for extending functionality.
methods.algorithm_summary: The Decorator pattern wraps an object in another object that adds behavior before or after delegating to the wrapped object. Decorators implement the same interface as the component they wrap. Multiple decorators can be stacked, each adding its own behavior. This provides a flexible alternative to subclassing, allowing behavior to be added at runtime.
assumptions: Dynamic extension is preferred over static inheritance
limitations: Many small objects can be hard to debug
Order of decoration can matter

references.paper_title: Design Patterns: Elements of Reusable Object-Oriented Software

methods.name: Facade
methods.short_description: Provide a unified interface to a set of interfaces in a subsystem, making the subsystem easier to use.
methods.algorithm_summary: The Facade pattern provides a simplified interface to a complex subsystem. The facade class delegates client requests to appropriate subsystem objects and may perform additional orchestration. Clients interact with the facade rather than the subsystem classes directly. This reduces coupling between clients and the subsystem, though clients can still access subsystem classes directly if needed.
assumptions: Subsystem is complex enough to warrant simplification
Most clients need only a subset of subsystem functionality
limitations: Can become a god object if not carefully managed
May limit access to subsystem power features

references.paper_title: Design Patterns: Elements of Reusable Object-Oriented Software

methods.name: Composite
methods.short_description: Compose objects into tree structures to represent part-whole hierarchies, letting clients treat individual objects and compositions uniformly.
methods.algorithm_summary: The Composite pattern allows you to compose objects into tree structures. A component interface declares operations common to both leaves and composites. Leaf nodes implement the interface directly, while composite nodes store children and delegate operations to them. This lets clients treat single objects and groups of objects uniformly through the component interface.
outputs: Uniform treatment of objects and compositions
assumptions: Clients should treat individual and composite objects uniformly
limitations: Can make design overly general
Hard to restrict components of a composite

references.paper_title: Design Patterns: Elements of Reusable Object-Oriented Software

methods.name: Proxy
methods.short_description: Provide a surrogate or placeholder for another object to control access to it.
methods.algorithm_summary: The Proxy pattern provides a surrogate object that controls access to the real subject. The proxy implements the same interface as the subject. Types include virtual proxy (lazy initialization), protection proxy (access control), remote proxy (network communication), and caching proxy (result caching). The proxy intercepts requests and may add behavior before or after forwarding them to the real subject.
outputs: Controlled access to the real subject
assumptions: Direct access to the real subject should be controlled
Client should not need to know about the proxy
limitations: Response time may increase due to indirection
Proxy implementation needs to stay in sync with the real subject

references.paper_title: Design Patterns: Elements of Reusable Object-Oriented Software

methods.name: Observer
methods.short_description: Define a one-to-many dependency between objects so that when one object changes state, all its dependents are notified and updated automatically.
methods.algorithm_summary: The Observer pattern establishes a subscription mechanism. The subject maintains a list of observers and provides methods to subscribe and unsubscribe. When the subject's state changes, it iterates over all registered observers and calls their update method. Observers can then query the subject for updated state. Push and pull variants determine whether the subject sends data or observers request it.
assumptions: Changes to one object require updating others
limitations: Can cause memory leaks if observers are not unsubscribed
Notification order is not guaranteed
Can cause cascading updates

references.paper_title: Design Patterns: Elements of Reusable Object-Oriented Software

methods.name: Strategy
methods.short_description: Define a family of algorithms, encapsulate each one, and make them interchangeable. Strategy lets the algorithm vary independently from clients that use it.
methods.algorithm_summary: The Strategy pattern encapsulates a family of algorithms behind a common interface. A context object holds a reference to a strategy object and delegates the algorithmic work to it. Clients can swap strategies at runtime by injecting a different implementation. This eliminates conditional statements for algorithm selection and makes it easy to add new algorithms without modifying existing code.
assumptions: Multiple algorithms exist for the same task
limitations: Clients must be aware of different strategies
Increased number of objects

references.paper_title: Design Patterns: Elements of Reusable Object-Oriented Software

methods.name: Template Method
methods.short_description: Define the skeleton of an algorithm in a method, deferring some steps to subclasses without changing the algorithm's structure.
methods.algorithm_summary: The Template Method pattern defines the skeleton of an algorithm in a base class method, with certain steps implemented as abstract or hook methods that subclasses override. The template method calls these steps in a fixed order, ensuring the algorithm's structure is preserved while allowing variation in specific steps. Hook methods provide optional extension points with default (often empty) implementations.
inputs: Abstract base class with template method
assumptions: The overall algorithm structure is fixed
Only certain steps need to vary
limitations: Inheritance-based, can be rigid
Violating Liskov substitution is easy
Limited runtime flexibility compared to Strategy

references.paper_title: Design Patterns: Elements of Reusable Object-Oriented Software

methods.name: Command
methods.short_description: Encapsulate a request as an object, thereby letting you parameterize clients with different requests, queue or log requests, and support undoable operations.
methods.algorithm_summary: The Command pattern encapsulates a request and its parameters as an object. An invoker holds a command and triggers its execution without knowing the receiver or the operation. Commands can be stored, queued, logged, and undone. The pattern decouples the object that invokes the operation from the one that knows how to perform it, enabling features like macro recording and transactional behavior.
assumptions: Operations need to be parameterized or deferred
limitations: Increases number of classes
Complex undo for stateful operations

references.paper_title: Design Patterns: Elements of Reusable Object-Oriented Software

methods.name: Iterator
methods.short_description: Provide a way to access the elements of an aggregate object sequentially without exposing its underlying representation.
methods.algorithm_summary: The Iterator pattern extracts the traversal behavior of a collection into a separate iterator object. The iterator keeps track of the current position and knows how to advance. The collection provides a method to create iterators. Different iterators can implement different traversal algorithms (forward, reverse, filtered) for the same collection. This separates collection management from traversal logic.
assumptions: Multiple traversal methods may be needed
limitations: Less efficient than direct access for simple collections
Built into most modern languages, making explicit pattern less common

references.paper_title: Design Patterns: Elements of Reusable Object-Oriented Software

methods.name: Mediator
methods.short_description: Define an object that encapsulates how a set of objects interact, promoting loose coupling by keeping objects from referring to each other explicitly.
methods.algorithm_summary: The Mediator pattern centralizes complex communication logic between objects into a single mediator object. Instead of objects communicating directly (creating a web of dependencies), they send messages to the mediator, which routes them appropriately. This transforms many-to-many relationships into one-to-many relationships, making the system easier to understand and modify.
limitations: Mediator can become a god object
Single point of failure

references.paper_title: Design Patterns: Elements of Reusable Object-Oriented Software

methods.name: State
methods.short_description: Allow an object to alter its behavior when its internal state changes, making the object appear to change its class.
methods.algorithm_summary: The State pattern lets an object behave differently depending on its current state. Each state is represented by a separate class implementing a common state interface. The context object delegates state-specific behavior to its current state object and allows state transitions by swapping the state object. This eliminates large conditional statements and makes adding new states straightforward.
assumptions: Object behavior depends on its state
limitations: Can be overkill for few states
State transitions can be scattered across state classes

references.paper_title: Design Patterns: Elements of Reusable Object-Oriented Software

methods.name: Visitor
methods.short_description: Represent an operation to be performed on the elements of an object structure, letting you define new operations without changing the classes of the elements.
methods.algorithm_summary: The Visitor pattern separates an algorithm from the object structure it operates on. Each element class implements an accept method that takes a visitor. The visitor has a visit method for each element type. When accept is called, it dispatches to the appropriate visit method (double dispatch). New operations can be added by creating new visitor classes without modifying element classes.
limitations: Hard to add new element types
Breaks encapsulation of element state
Complex double dispatch mechanism

references.paper_title: Design Patterns: Elements of Reusable Object-Oriented Software

methods.name: Thread Pool
methods.short_description: Manage a pool of reusable threads to execute tasks, reducing the overhead of thread creation and destruction.
methods.algorithm_summary: The Thread Pool pattern maintains a collection of pre-created threads waiting for tasks. A task queue holds submitted work items. Idle threads pick up tasks from the queue and execute them. After completing a task, the thread returns to the pool. This amortizes the cost of thread creation, controls the degree of concurrency, and prevents resource exhaustion from unbounded thread creation.
limitations: Fixed pool size may not suit all workloads
Task starvation possible with priority-less queues

references.paper_title: Java Concurrency in Practice

methods.name: Producer-Consumer
methods.short_description: Decouple the production of data from its consumption using a shared buffer, allowing producers and consumers to operate at different rates.
methods.algorithm_summary: The Producer-Consumer pattern uses a shared bounded buffer between producer and consumer threads. Producers add items to the buffer when space is available; consumers remove items when the buffer is non-empty. Synchronization primitives (mutexes, semaphores, condition variables) coordinate access. The buffer decouples production and consumption rates, enabling asynchronous processing and load balancing.
assumptions: Producers and consumers operate at different rates
limitations: Buffer overflow or underflow handling needed
Deadlock risk with incorrect synchronization

references.paper_title: Java Concurrency in Practice
